pages.text: Inﬂuence of supplementary cementitious materials on engineering properties of high strength concrete M.A. Megat Johari a,⇑, J.J. Brooks b, Shahid Kabir a, Patrice Rivard c a School of Civil Engineering, Universiti Sains Malaysia, Malaysia b School of Civil Engineering, University of Leeds, United Kingdom c University of Sherbrooke, Sherbrooke, Quebec, Canada article info Article history: Received 24 May 2009 Received in revised form 27 November 2010 Accepted 7 December 2010 Available online 7 January 2011 Keywords: Supplementary cementitious materials Workability Strength Elastic modulus Porosity Pore size distribution High strength concrete abstract The inﬂuence of supplementary cementitious materials (SCMs), namely silica fume, metakaolin, ﬂy ash and ground granulated blast-furnace slag, on the engineering properties of high strength concrete (HSC) has been investigated in this study. Workability, compressive strength, elastic modulus, porosity and pore size distribution were assessed in order to quantify the effects of the different materials. The results show that the inclusion of the different SCMs has considerable inﬂuence on the workability of HSC. Silica fume and metakaolin signiﬁcantly enhanced the strength of HSC. Fly ash reduced the early- age strength; however, it enhanced the long-term strength of the HSC. Likewise, ground granulated blast-furnace slag impaired the early-age strength, but marginally improved the long-term strength at low replacement levels. The general effect of the different SCMs on the elastic modulus of HSC is rather small compared to their effect on strength. There are good correlations between both static and dynamic moduli and compressive strength. The EC 2 and ACI 209 provide a good estimate of static modulus of elasticity from compressive strength, while the BS8110 gives a good estimate of static modulus of elas- ticity from dynamic modulus of HSC containing the different SCMs. Porosity and pore size were reduced with the addition of the different SCMs. The volume of mesopores in the ranges of <15 nm and 15 – 30 nm was notably increased for HSC containing SCMs, whereas the percentage of macropores was signiﬁcantly reduced. /C2112010 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction The use of supplementary cementitious materials (SCMs) such as ﬂy ash, ground granulated blast-furnace slag, silica fume and metakaolin as part of binders for concrete has been increasing throughout the world, particularly in the production of high strength and high performance concrete. This is due to the potential ability of these materials to enhance the properties and performance of concrete through their ﬁller effect, as well as pozzolanic reaction. In addition, most of the SCMs are by-product materials; hence, their inclusion in concrete could serve as an effective means of disposal. Furthermore, their utilization as mineral admixtures to partially re- place cement could somehow preserve the non-renewable re- sources required for the production of cement, and hence could somehow contribute to sustainable concrete construction [1–3]. Metakaolin is the most recent SCM to be commercially intro- duced to the concrete construction industry. Unlike other SCMs, metakaolin is not an industrial by-product; it is produced by cal- cining high purity kaolin clay at temperatures of 700–800 /C176C. It has been shown that the inclusion of metakaolin could improve the properties and durability performance of concrete [3–6]. The in

This paper presents the results of an investigation into the inﬂu- ence of supplementary cementitious materials (SCMs) on the engi- neering properties of high strength concrete (HSC) with 28-days cube compressive strength in excess of 80 MPa. The workability, compressive strength, elastic modulus, porosity and pore size dis- tribution of HSC containing different proportions of SCMs, namely silica fume (SF), metakaolin (MK), ﬂy ash (FA) and ground granu- lated blast-furnace slag (GGBS), are compared with those of a con- trol HSC mix having identical mix proportions but without SCMs. Hence, the study will provide comparison on the effects of the dif- ferent SCMs on properties of HSC and enlighten the potential of the SCMs with regard to their application in high strength and high performance concrete. In addition, the available standards indicate that proper provision on the potential inﬂuence of the different SCMs on the estimation of elastic modulus is currently lacking or not available. Hence, the paper also attempts at evaluating the suitability of several available standards in predicting the moduli of elasticity of the HSC containing the different SCMs. 2. Materials and mix proportions The cementitious materials used were Ordinary Portland Cement (OPC), SF, MK, FA and GGBS. The chemical compositions and the physical properties of the cemen- titious materials are given in Table 1. Details of the mix proportions for the concrete containing SCMs are given in Table 2. Natural river sand and quartzite natural grav- el with a nominal maximum size of 10 mm were used as the aggregates. The control mix was cast using OPC, while the other mixes were prepared by replacing part of the cement with the different SCMs at three different replacement levels on a mass-for-mass basis. An optimum water/cement ratio of 0.28 was obtained for the control HSC mix and the same water/binder ratio was used for the other con- crete mixes with the same amount of superplasticiser so that the workability changes due to the effect of the different SCM. The superplasticiser used is based on sulphonated vinyl copolymer. 3. Test procedures 3.1. Workability The term workability is broadly deﬁned; no single test method measures all aspects of workability. The ACI Committee 116 [15] describes workability as ‘‘that property of freshly mixed concrete or mortar that determines the ease with which it can be mixed, placed, consolidated, and ﬁnished to a homogenous mass’’. Sufﬁ- cient workability is necessary so that the concrete can be placed and compacted to maximum density. In this study, the workability of the fresh concrete was measured by means of the slump test in accordance to BS EN 12350-2 [16]. 3.2. Compressive strength Compressive strength test determines behavior of materials un- der compressive loads; the specimen is compressed and the max- imum sustained load is recorded. In this study, the compressive strength test was performed on 100 mm cube specimens at the age of 1, 3, 7, 14, 28, 90, 180 and 365 days. Three specimens were tested at each testing age and the average strength was reported. The compressive strength was determined in accordance to BS EN 12390-3 [17]. The specimens were tested for compressive strength using a 3000 kN capacity concrete compression machine. 3.3. Static and dynamic moduli of elasticity The static modulus of elasticity was measured on 100 /C2 100 /C2 500 mm prism specimens according to BS 1881: Part 121 [18]. Strain was measured on t

of the specimen. Knowing the resonance frequency, the dynamic modulus was calculated using the following equation: Ed ¼ 4 /C2 n2L2 /C2 q /C2 10/C0 15 ð1Þ where Ed is the dynamic modulus of elasticity (GPa), n is resonant frequency (Hz), L is length of specimen (mm), q is the density of specimen (kg/m 3). 3.4. Porosity and pore size distribution The porosity and pore size distribution of the high strength mortars were assessed using the Mercury Intrusion Porosimetry (MIP) apparatus. Mortar samples obtained by wet sieving of fresh concrete were used for the measurement of porosity and pore size distribution. The samples were tested at the age of 28 days. At the age of testing, cylindrical cores of 20-mm diameter were extracted from 50-mm thick mortar slabs. The two ends of the cored samples were cut and discarded to ensure homogeneity of samples and the remaining piece was cut to about 20 mm long. Before testing, the samples were dried in an oven at 105 ± 5 /C176C for 24 h. This removed volatile materials such as adsorbed water, which evaporate during the vacuum procedure and may prolong the time to achieve the de- sired vacuum reading prior to mercury intrusion. 4. Results and discussion 4.1. Inﬂuence of SCMs on workability of HSC The results of the slump test are given in Table 2 . From the re- sults, it is clear that the different SCMs used in the concrete mixes inﬂuence the workability of concrete. The effect of replacing part of the cement with 5% and 10% SF is that the workability of HSC improves; however, there is a decreas- ing effect as replacement levels of SF increase from 5% to 10%. On the other hand, at a higher replacement level of 15%, there is a mar- ginal reduction in workability compared to the OPC concrete. The improvement in workability at lower contents of SF could be ex- plained as due to the combined effect of superplasticiser as well as the particle characteristic of SF. The superplasticiser improved workability by dispersing the cement and SF particles through its adsorption and electrostatic repulsion mechanisms. At the same time, the SF which is known for its extreme ﬁneness and having spherical shape could provide additional water reduction through its potential ball-bearing effect [20], similar to the case of ﬂy ash. Nonetheless, in the case of SF15 concrete mix, the marginal reduc- tion in workability could be due to the high content of SF that off- set the potential water-reducing effect of superplasticiser and SF. Hence, this seems to suggest that there could be an optimum SF content which will provide maximum dispersion of cement and SF, contributing to water reduction and higher workability. Above this optimum content, the extremely higher ﬁneness of SF will be dominant, resulting in increase water demand and lower workability. In the case of MK concrete, Table 2 shows that there is an enor- mous reduction in workability, as part of the cement is replaced by MK. In fact, the results also indicate that the degree of workability exhibited by the MK concrete mixes range from low to very low as the replacement level increases. This shows that the addition of MK signiﬁcantly increases the water demand of concrete. This can be attributed to the high surface area of the MK. Although the MK concrete mixes exhibited a lower degree of workability compared to the other concrete mixes, compaction of the concrete was done successfully without any problem. These ﬁndings are generally in agreement with previous ﬁndings

dependent on the rate of OPC hydration. In the case of HSC mixes containing SCMs, their strength development should be dependent on the rate of OPC hydration as well as the reaction between the SCMs and calcium hydroxide, Ca(OH) 2. Therefore, the inﬂuence of the SCMs on the strength development relative to the control mix could be quantiﬁed from the relative strength-time plots. The inﬂuences of each SCM are discussed in detail in the following sub-sections. 4.2.1. Inﬂuence of SF Table 3 and Fig. 1 show that in comparison to the control mix, the effect of SF is to enhance the strength of the HSC at all ages. In fact, the results demonstrate that the inclusion of SF as partial OPC replacement provided greater strength enhancement than the other SCMs. The early strength development of the SF concrete is greater than that of the control. Even at the age of 1 day, the rel- ative strength of the SF concrete is 109.5%, 108.6% and 107.4% for SF5, SF10 and SF15, respectively. The greater early strength devel- opment could be attributed to the combined inﬂuence of an accel- eration in OPC hydration and the ‘‘microﬁller effect’’ as part of the cement was replaced by SF. According to Detwiler and Mehta [26], due to the extreme ﬁneness, SF particles provide nucleation sites for calcium silicate hydrate, C–S–H, and Ca(OH) 2. In addition, the ultraﬁne SF particles act as microﬁller, densifying the transition Table 3 Compressive strength of HSC mixes at different testing ages (N/mm 2). literatures on the general effect of mineral admixtures on pore structures of concrete [5,9,37]. In general, the reduction in porosity and the reﬁnement of pore size as a result of the inclusion of the different SCMs could have signiﬁcant inﬂuence on strength, dura- bility performance as well as deformation characteristics of con- crete [5,9,11,12]. 5. Conclusions From the results presented earlier in this paper, the following conclusions can be made: 1. The use of SF at a replacement level of up to 10% tends to improve the workability of HSC. From the literature, this sce- nario has been observed only in concrete produced with low water/binder ratio in conjunction with the use of superplasticis- er. The effect of MK is to reduce the workability of HSC with greater reducing effects at higher replacement levels. The inﬂu- ence of FA and GGBS on the workability of HSC is the same as that observed in normal strength concrete, which is to improve the workability. Greater effects were observed at higher replacement levels. 2. The inclusion of the different SCMs signiﬁcantly inﬂuences the compressive strength of the HSC mixes. The effect of SF is to enhance the compressive strength of the concretes at all ages, particularly between the ages of 28 and 90 days. Similarly, the general effect of MK is to enhance the strength of the HSC, except for the MK15, concrete at the age of 1 day. At a replace- ment level of 5%, the maximum contribution of MK to the HSC strength occurs at the age of 1 day, while at higher replacement levels of 10% and 15%, the maximum contribution to strength takes place between the ages of 14 and 28 days. Due to the slower reactivity of FA and GGBS, as well as the dilution effect, their inclusion reduces the early strength of the concrete, par- ticularly at higher replacement levels. While the FA enhances the later age strength of the HSC, the GGBS gives maximum strength between the ages of 28 and 90 days. For each mineral admixture, SF, MK, FA and GGBS, maximum long-term strength was obtained at replacement levels of 15%, 10%, 30% and 20%, respectively. 3. The general effect of the different SCMs on elastic modulus of HSC is nominal compared to their effect on strength. The static modulus of elasticity of the HSC at the age of 28 days can be expressed as a function of compressive strength. The EC 2 and ACI 209 models give good estimates of static modulus of elastic- ity. The equation given in BS8110 tends to slightly underesti- mate the static modulus of elasticity, while the ACI 363 tends to underestimate static modulus of elasticity with higher error coefﬁcient. 4. The dynamic modulus of elasticity of the HSC can be expressed as a function of the cube compressive strength raised to the power of 0.5. The general relation between static and dynamic moduli of elasticity, as given in BS 8110, could be applicable to the HSC, with or without SCMs. 5. The effect of SCM is to reduce the porosity of mortar measured using the MIP apparatus, while the mean and average pore sizes are signiﬁcantly reduced for all mortars containing SCMs. SF and MK seem to have greater inﬂuences in reducing the poros- ity and pore size of mortar. 6. The effect of the SCMs is to increase the volume of mesopores in the ranges of <15 nm and 15–30 nm, but to signiﬁcantly reduce the percentage of macropores. The mortars containing SF and MK, as well as the GGBS60 mortar, show signiﬁcant increase in the percentage of mesopores in the range of <15 nm, with

SCMs on the static and dynamic moduli of elasticity of the HSC is not as signiﬁcant as their effect on compressive strength, despite the apparent general trend for higher strength to lead to higher elastic modulus. 4.3.1. Relationship between static modulus and strength The static modulus of elasticity at the age of 28 days is pre- sented as a function of cube compressive strength in Fig. 5 .I ti s obvious that the relationship between static modulus and com- pressive strength is adequately represented by Eq. (2), with a coef- ﬁcient of determination, ( R2) of greater than 0.85. This shows that there is strong correlation between compressive strength and sta- tic modulus of elasticity. Es ¼ 12:184 f 0:287 c ð2Þ where Es is the static modulus of elasticity (GPa), fc is compressive strength (MPa). Fig. 5 also shows that the equation given in BS 8110 [29] slightly underestimates the static modulus of elasticity of HSC, with a rea- sonable error coefﬁcient of 7.9% ( Table 5 ). Here, the error coefﬁ- cient was determined as follows: M ¼ 100 /C3 1 Xm ﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃPðXp /C0 XmÞ2 n /C0 1 s0 @ 1 A ð3Þ where M is the error coefﬁcient (%), Xm is average of measured val- ues, Xp is predicted values, Xm is measured values, n is the number of observations. In Fig. 6 , the experimental data for the relationship between static modulus and compressive strength are compared with prediction models, such as those described in EC 2 [30], ACI 209 [31] and ACI 363 [32]. The measured values of static modulus and those estimated by the different prediction models, as well as the error coefﬁcients, are given in Table 5 . From Fig. 6 and Ta- ble 5 , it is clear that the EC 2 [30] and ACI 209 [31] models give good estimates of the static modulus, with an error coefﬁcient of 3.2% and 5.6%, respectively. On the other hand, the ACI 363 [32] tends to underestimate the static modulus of elasticity with a much higher error coefﬁcient of 18.7%. 4.3.2. Relationship between dynamic modulus and strength In Fig. 7 , the dynamic modulus of elasticity was plotted as a function of cube compressive strength raised to the power of 0.5. It is clear that the dynamic modulus of elasticity can be related to compressive strength by the following equation: Ed ¼ 3:57 f 1=2 c þ 16:6 ð4Þ where Ed is the dynamic modulus of elasticity (GPa), fc is compres- sive strength (MPa). The coefﬁcient of determination, ( R2) obtained for Eq. (4) is 0.92. This high value of R2 indicates that the dynamic modulus of elastic- ity of the HSC can be successfully expressed as a function of cube compressive strength by Eq. (4). Earlier, Brooks and Wainwright [33] found that the relationship between dynamic modulus of elas- ticity and cube strength of HSC with 28 days cube strength ranging from 74 to 94 MPa, is adequately described by the equation given in CP110 [34], which is as follows: Ed ¼ 2:8 f 1=2 c þ 22 ð5Þ Table 4 Measured static and dynamic moduli of elasticity at the age of 28 days. Consequently, in Fig. 7, the measured dynamic modulus of elasticity was plotted as a function of cube compressive strength together with Eqs. (4) and (5) . In comparison to the measured values of dy- namic modulus, Eq. (5) appears to slightly underestimate the dy- namic modulus of the HSC particularly at the higher range of compressive strength, with an acceptable error coefﬁcient of 5%. 4.3.3. Relationship between static and dynamic moduli The relationship between static and dynamic moduli of elastic- ity is shown in Fig. 8. It is clear that the static modulus of elasticity could be related to the dynamic modulus of elasticity by Eq. (6) as follows: Es ¼ 0:88 Ed ð6Þ where Es is the static modulus (GPa), Ed is dynamic modulus (GPa). Here, the coefﬁcient of determination, ( R2) yielded a reasonably high value of 0.83, indicating the potentially signiﬁcant statistical correlation between the two elastic moduli. Earlier, Lyndon and Balendran [35] proposed a relationship between static and dy- namic moduli of elasticity of plain concrete as given by Eq. (7). Nonetheless, the equation tends to underestimate static modulus of elasticity with an error coefﬁcient of 6%. Es ¼ 0:83 Ed ð7Þ In BS 8110 [29], a general relation between static and dynamic moduli of elasticity for concretes containing not more than 500 kg/m3 of cement is given as the following: Es ¼ 1:25 Ed /C0 19 ð8Þ The measured values of the static modulus of elasticity at the age of 28 days are compared those estimated using Eqs. (7) and (8) in Fig. 8 together with the upper and lower limits (±4 GPa) of Eq. (8), as given in the Code of Practice. It can be seen that all points fall within these limits. In addition, the calculated value of error coefﬁ- cient for the static modulus of elasticity determined using Eq. (8) gives a very low value of about 3%. This shows that the equation can be used to estimate the static modulus from the dynamic mod- ulus of elasticity of HSC containing SCMs. Nonetheless, the EC 2 rec- ommends that the elastic modulus should be speciﬁcally assessed if the structure is likely to be sensitive to deviation from the esti- mated values [30]. 4.4. Porosity and pore size distribution The summary of the MIP test results, i.e. values of porosity, median pore diameter, average pore diameter, and total intrusion volume is tabulated in Table 6.I n Table 7, the pore size of the mor- tars is divided into several ranges of size from <15 nm to greater than 50 nm to assess the inﬂuence of the different SCMs on pore size distribution. The effect of the SCMs on the porosity, pore size and pore size distribution of high strength mortar is discussed in the following sub-sections. 4.4.1. Effect of SCM on porosity and pore size From Table 6 , it is evident that the effect of the SCMs is that they reduce the porosity of the high strength mortars. In compar- ison to the OPC mortar, the mortars containing SCMs exhibited a reduction in porosity of between 9.7% and 37%, with the FA30 and SF15 showing the lowest and highest reduction, respectively. At the same replacement levels, SF seems to be more effective in reducing porosity than MK, while GGBS is more effective than FA. Increasing the replacement levels of SF and MK reduces the porosity of mortar, but the effect of FA and GGBS replacement levels is rather inconsistent. Table 6 also gives the value of total intrusion volume for every mortar sample. The total intrusion vol- ume is independent of the shape and size of the pores;

zone, thus, enhancing the matrix-aggregate bond and increasing the concrete strength [26,27]. Fig. 1 clearly shows that the relative strength starts to increase signiﬁcantly after the age of 3 days, which seems to suggest that the onset of pozzolanic reaction com- mences from about 3 days. The maximum contribution of SF in enhancing the strength of the HSC took place between the ages of 28 and 90 days. The relative strength of SF5, SF10 and SF15 at 28 days was 122%, 131%, and 136%, respectively. This signiﬁcant increase in strength could be due to the pozzolanic reaction be- tween SF and Ca(OH) 2 to produce C–S–H. As more Ca(OH) 2 is con- verted to C–S–H, the strength of the concrete will continue to increase. According to Hanehara et al. [28], the onset of pozzolanic reaction in a cement-SF system normally takes place after 3– 4 days, and this is conﬁrmed by the results of the present study. It has also been reported that about 24% of SF by weight of OPC is required to eliminate the Ca(OH) 2 content in the hardened SF- OPC paste system [20]. Therefore, as presented in Fig. 1 , the con- crete mixes containing SF particularly at higher replacement levels provided greater strength development due to pozzolanic reaction. After about 90 days of moist curing, the strength development seems to reduce, which could be attributed to the depletion of SF. Although the long-term relative strength of the SF concretes tends to reduce, the actual strength is still signiﬁcantly higher than that of the control mix. The 1-year strength for SF5, SF10, and SF15, respectively, was 17%, 21%, and 22% higher than that of the OPC mix. Therefore, between the three replacement levels, the SF15 concrete exhibits a maximum long-term strength. However, the long-term strength of the SF15 concrete is only marginally higher than that of SF10. 4.2.2. Inﬂuence of MK In the same manner as with the SF, the general effect of MK is to improve the strength of the HSC, except for the MK15 concrete at the early age of 1 day ( Table 3 and Fig. 2). For the MK5 concrete, a maximum relative strength of 114% occurred at the age of 1 day, which then reduced with curing time to 101% at 1 year. The same trend was observed by Wild et al. [6], and they attributed the early enhancement in strength mainly to the initial acceleration in OPC hydration, an effect that is lost with prolonged curing time. Since the 5% replacement level is rather small, the dilution effect, ﬁller effect and contribution by pozzolanic reaction are expected to be very minimal. Here, dilution effect can be explained by a reduction in cement content as part of the cement is replaced by MK. As the replacement level of MK is increased to 10% and 15%, there is a consistent reduction in relative strength at the early age of 1 and 3 days when compared to those of the MK5 concrete, where a greater reduction was noticed at higher replacement levels. The relative strength of the MK10 and MK15 at the age of 1 day is 105% and 98%, respectively. This reduction could be attributed to the dilution effect as higher amounts of cement are replaced by MK. For the concrete containing 10% and 15% MK, the maximum rel- ative strength takes place between 14 and 28 days of curing time. This seems to suggest that the maximum contribution by pozzola- nic reaction for these concrete mixes occurs between the ages of 14 and 28 days. With prolonged curing time, the relative strength de- creases and tends to approach a constant value after 180 d
